name: Homemade Dahi (Curd / Yogurt)
description: The simplest and most essential fermented dairy in Indian cooking — full-fat milk cultured with a live starter until thick and tangy. Once you make your own, you'll never buy it again.

ingredient_quantities.curd starter: 2 tbsp live curd (from previous batch or store)

instructions: Heat milk in a heavy-bottomed pan to a full boil, stirring to prevent skin.
Cool the milk until it is just warm enough to hold a finger in comfortably — 40–45°C. Don't let it go cold.
Transfer to a ceramic or glass bowl. Add the starter curd; whisk gently to incorporate.
Cover with a lid or plate. Place in a warm spot: inside an oven with just the light on, or wrapped in a thick towel.
Do not disturb for 6–8 hours (overnight in summer, 8–10 hours in winter).
Once set with a firm wobble, refrigerate for at least 2 hours to stop fermentation and firm up further.
tips: Temperature is everything — too hot and the starter dies, too cold and it won't set. 42°C is ideal. Always save 2 tbsp from each batch to set the next.

name: Homemade Coconut Milk
description: Fresh coconut milk extracted from grated coconut yields a richness and flavour unmatched by any can — the essential base for Kerala curries, payasam, and South Asian sweets.

ingredient_quantities.warm water: 2 cups for first press + 2 cups for second press

instructions: Grate the coconut finely using a box grater or food processor.

First press (thick milk): Add 1 cup warm water to grated coconut. Knead and squeeze with your hands for 2 minutes. Strain through a muslin cloth, pressing hard to extract all liquid. This is thick coconut milk.
Second press (thin milk): Return the coconut pulp to the bowl. Add 1 cup more warm water. Squeeze again. This thinner milk is used for cooking.
Repeat the second press with another cup of water for a third, lighter extraction if needed.
Use fresh coconut milk within 24 hours. Refrigerate or freeze in portions.
tips: Thick milk is added at the end of cooking (never boiled); thin milk is used to simmer. Don't microwave — it separates. A Preethi coconut scraper or blender attachment speeds up grating considerably.

name: Cashew Cream and Paste
description: A versatile dairy-free cream made from soaked and blended raw cashews — the secret behind silky vegan curries, creamy pasta sauces, and glossy Mughlai gravies.

ingredient_quantities.raw cashews: 1 cup, soaked 2–4 hours (or 20 min in boiling water)
ingredient_quantities.water: 1/3 cup for paste, 1/2 cup for cream

instructions: Drain and rinse soaked cashews thoroughly.
For cashew paste: blend cashews with 1/3 cup water until completely smooth and thick. Stop to scrape down the sides several times. Blend 3–5 minutes for silky result.
For cashew cream: blend with 1/2 cup water for a pourable consistency similar to single cream.
Strain through a fine mesh sieve for an ultra-smooth result if using in delicate sauces.
Store refrigerated up to 5 days, or freeze in ice cube trays.
tips: The longer you soak, the creamier the result. A high-powered blender makes a world of difference. Use in place of cream in Mughlai recipes, or as a thickener in gravies.

name: Birista (Crispy Fried Onions)
description: Golden, paper-thin fried onion shreds — the essential garnish for biryani, dal, and kebabs that also dissolves into gravies to add sweetness, colour, and depth.
instructions: Slice onions paper-thin using a mandoline or sharp knife. Separate all the rings.
Optional: spread slices on a tray, sprinkle a pinch of salt, and let sit 10 minutes. Pat very dry with paper towels — moisture is the enemy of crisp.
Heat oil to 160°C in a wide, deep pan. Add onion slices in batches — don't crowd.
Fry on medium-low, stirring occasionally, until the sizzling slows and onions turn golden-amber, 15–20 minutes.
Remove immediately with a slotted spoon (they continue cooking after removal). Spread on paper towels.
They crisp further as they cool — leave undisturbed for 5 minutes. Store in a dry airtight container up to 2 weeks.
tips: Pull the birista out one shade lighter than you want — they always darken slightly after removal. Drying the onions completely beforehand makes them crisp, not greasy.

name: Ginger Garlic Paste
description: The fragrant foundation of almost every Indian curry, marinade, and dal — an equal-part blend of fresh ginger and garlic ground into a smooth, pungent paste that keeps for weeks.
instructions: Peel garlic cloves and roughly chop peeled ginger into chunks.
Blend garlic and ginger together with oil and salt until smooth, scraping down sides as needed.
Add a tablespoon of water if the blender struggles to move.
Continue blending until the paste is completely smooth with no fibrous bits.
Transfer to a clean glass jar. Top with a thin layer of oil to seal.
Refrigerate and use within 3–4 weeks. Alternatively, freeze in 1-tbsp portions in an ice cube tray.
tips: Equal parts ginger and garlic is the classic ratio. Adding turmeric (1 tsp per batch) makes it an all-purpose curry base ready to go. The salt and oil layer extend shelf life considerably.
